title: A list of pairs
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the specs instead of fixing them. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-closed: <dt> and <dd> close like everything else. Four pairs means eight tags and eight closings.
hint3.not-closed: The <dl> closes too — last of all, after the final </dd>.
why.no-dt: There are not four names. <dt> is the name: "Frame", "Wheels", "Gears", "Weight". One for each pair.
hint2.no-dt: Three tags: <dl> on the outside, and inside you alternate <dt> and <dd>.
hint3.no-dt: <dl><dt>Colour</dt><dd>Red</dd></dl> — one pair. The name, then the value.
hint4.no-dt: For each of the four rows: the name goes in a <dt>, the value in a <dd>. All four pairs inside one <dl>.
why.no-dd: There are names, but not four values. <dd> is the answer: "Aluminium", "26 inches", "21", "12 kg".
hint2.no-dd: Every <dt> wants a <dd> right after it.
hint3.no-dd: <dt>Colour</dt><dd>Red</dd> — you ask and you answer. Asking with no answer is no use.
why.empty-dt: There is an empty name. An empty <dt> asks nothing.
why.empty-dd: There is an empty value. An empty <dd> answers nothing.
why.still-ul: The old <ul> is still there. You are not adding the pairs NEXT TO it — you are replacing it with them.
hint2.still-ul: Search for <ul> and <li> in your code. Not one should remain.
hint3.still-ul: A list of four things and a list of four pairs say different things. They cannot both stand.
why.kept-colon: You kept the colons. "Frame: aluminium" inside a <dt> is the old thinking with new tags. The colon was your way of separating name from value — the tags do that now. You do not need it any more.
hint2.kept-colon: Look at your <dt>. Is there anything in it besides the name?
hint3.kept-colon: <dt>Frame:</dt> — the colon is a leftover from the <li>. Here it separates nothing, it just hangs there.
hint4.kept-colon: Remove every colon. The <dt> holds only the name, the <dd> only the value.
why.lost-rows: A row is gone. The four pairs are frame, wheels, gears and weight. The task is to describe them more precisely, not to shorten them.
hint2.lost-rows: Count the pairs in the result. There should be four.
blocks.0.text: You are adding the bike's specifications. Every line is a pair: a name and a value. Frame — aluminium. Wheels — 26 inches.
blocks.1.text: You did it with a list and a colon. It works. But look at what your code says: "here are four things of one kind". They are not four things. They are four PAIRS. The colon is something only you know about.
blocks.2.text: There is a tag for exactly this
blocks.3.text: <dl> — a description list. Inside: <dt> is the name (the term), <dd> is the value (the description).
blocks.7.text: Specifications. A glossary. Questions and answers. Metadata — author, date, category. Anywhere there is a name and an answer.
blocks.8.text: One name can have several values — you just put two <dd> in a row. And the other way round: two names sharing one value.
blocks.9.text: Notice the browser indents the values by itself. Again, you never told it to. And notice one more thing: the colon is gone. You needed it while you had nothing to separate name from value with. Now the tags do it.
blocks.11.text: Google reads <dl> and understands this is a spec — it can show it straight in the results. From a <ul> with colons it understands nothing.
blocks.12.text: The same on screen. Different for everything else.
